data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Tight Spreads Keep A Lid On Net Income
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up, highlighting individual news and events-as well as the top-down-providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the first week of August.

Market Action

BDCs had a tough week, underperforming the broader income market. This was despite strong early earnings numbers from the sector. All names in our coverage were lower, with PSEC falling by double-digit figures. Year-to-date, the average stock in our coverage is flat, though dispersion is very high.

The average valuation in our coverage fell below 100% and is a few percentage points below the longer-term average figure.

Market Themes

One interesting piece of data in the Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX) Q2 earnings release was the weighted-average spread of new commitments. This was 6.7% in Q2, below the 7% level in Q1 which was the highest over the previous year.

This, admittedly anecdotal piece of evidence, suggests that the market volatility in April was much too brief to allow BDCs to lend at favorable spreads. With short-term rates having been flat for some time, secondary factors like lending spreads, leverage, and interest expense have come to the fore in driving BDC net income figures. The fact that spreads are relatively tight, if not at the lowest historical levels, suggests that net income will continue to face headwinds.

Other evidence suggests that current BDC sector spreads are below portfolio weighted-average levels. This means that as loans are repaid and BDCs make new loans, the overall level of net income falls slightly. This will not have a huge impact on BDC net income, however, until the Fed makes its next move, it will continue to weigh on income levels in the sector.

Market Commentary

Hercules Capital (HTGC) delivered stellar results with a 6.9% quarterly return. The NAV jumped 2.5% - one of the strongest rises in the last few years.

There was a chunky net realized loss; however, it was more than made up by unrealized appreciation. Unrealized appreciation remains a key theme of this young earnings season, boosting overall returns in the sector. Leverage remained modest, below 1x with low non-accruals at just 0.2%.

Ares Capital Corp.'s (ARCC) core EPS came in unchanged from Q1. The NAV rose 0.4% for a total NAV return of 2.9% for the quarter. This was above the +2.1% Q1 figure but below the prior two quarters. Overall, a very solid double-digit annualized number.

Dividend coverage was 104%, and this will move below 100% if the Fed continues to deliver the 3-4 rate cuts that the market expects. The most recent payrolls number suggests we will see another cut from the Fed in September.

The company has quite a bit of spillover left, so it will try to avoid cutting the dividend if it can. Non-accruals rose slightly to 1.2% on fair-value from 0.9%. Overall, a very good result. The stock is fairly expensive at a 13% premium - 20% above the median figure - the highest in a long time.

Stance And Takeaways

Though the Q2 earnings season has only begun and we have only seen the stronger BDCs report, so far there is good reason for cautious optimism. As we suggested earlier, the tightening in public credit spreads during the quarter should support NAVs via unrealized appreciation, and this is what we are seeing. This means that despite the headwinds of tight spreads, falling short-term rates, and a low level of M&A activity, BDCs continue to deliver very attractive returns for investors while limiting their exposure to higher longer-term rates.

data.title: 3 Super Popular Stocks I Wouldn't Touch With A 10-Foot Pole
data.description:
Introduction

Every now and then, people ask me why I only cover stocks I like.

While that's a very valid question, the answer is very straightforward, as I believe one's time is much better spent finding the right investments than the ones to avoid.

It's not even close, actually.

Why?

That's based on the fact that the overwhelming majority of global stocks simply aren't that great. According to fascinating research from Arizona State University's Hendrik Bessembinder (and his team), who studied the lifetime returns of every U.S. common stock in the United States since 1926, just 86 companies accounted for $16 trillion in wealth generation, roughly half of the market's total wealth generation.

As you can imagine, the list is led by Big Tech among some of the well-known dividend growers you may or may not have in your portfolio:

Even better (or worse), the remaining 96% of stocks collectively matched one-month T-bills, which means they were barely better than cash and equivalents.

To quote the research, "the majority of individual stock investments led to decreased rather than increased wealth in the long run." Moreover, "Aggregate shareholder wealth creation is concentrated in a relatively few high-performing stocks."

That's pretty shocking, isn't it?

Now, there's no reason to panic. Although these numbers may indicate that anyone who tries to be a stockpicker will fail miserably, we need to be aware that North America is home to more than 10,000 publicly listed companies.

Assuming 96% will continue to be a bad investment, it would still leave us with more than 400 companies that are, statistically speaking, able to generate substantial long-term wealth. Given that I alone cover way more than 100 stocks, I think that's a reasonable assumption.

Also, given how much time and energy we spend in finding the investments that are likely to keep generating wealth, I think the odds are certainly in our favor.

Nonetheless, I think it's sometimes a great exercise to look into stocks to avoid. That's what this article is about. However, instead of giving you some random stocks from "the 96%" you wouldn't buy anyway, I decided to go with some very popular stocks that, I believe, are likely to report disappointing long-term returns.

Needless to say, I hold no short positions in any of these companies, and I do not benefit in any way if my theses are correct. It's just food for thought. So, even if you own the stocks I'm about to mention, see it as me playing devil's advocate rather than trying to persuade you to sell your investments.

So, let's get to it!

Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (ARE): 7% Yield In A Business Environment I Simply Don't Trust

The first idea is a pick that could bite me in the [you know where] if I'm wrong, as this healthcare REIT is trading at 2015 levels, a mile below its all-time high. If it recovers, I could look like an idiot saying it's an "avoid" at $77.

However, I'm willing to take that risk.

For starters, I don't hate Alexandria. I think it has good management and a history of consistent dividend growth, something not every REIT can say. Currently, it yields 7.0% with a healthy payout ratio of less than 60%.

In fact, I have been bullish on the company in the past.

What bothers me is the business environment.

See, Alexandria is classified as an office REIT. However, it doesn't own random offices but advanced biolaboratories. Among its tenants are some of the biggest pharmaceutical companies on the planet, who all need laboratories to discover new drugs that address healthcare needs and provide them with future growth to offset patent losses.

Most of these tenants have investment-grade balance sheets. This also goes for Alexandria, which enjoys a BBB+ rating (one step below the A range), an average weighted rate of just 4.0% on its debt, an average debt duration until maturity of 12.0 years, nearly $5.0 billion in liquidity, and a net leverage ratio close to 5.0x adjusted EBITDA.

So, why is the company selling off?

The problem is that even the healthcare office market is under pressure.

As we can see below, between Q2'24 and Q2'25, the occupancy rate in North America fell from 94.6% to 90.8%. Including redevelopment properties, that number is close to 86.0%. These numbers are surprisingly poor for a company that deals with some of the safest tenants on the planet.

With that said, as you may know, the office space is in trouble.

Ever since the pandemic, office vacancies have exploded, fueled by work-from-home and the general realization that the past few decades saw too much office supply growth.

While Alexandria does not own random offices, it is increasingly competing, as office owners are turning their offices into value-added facilities like laboratories.

Owners of offices found out just how attractive the life sciences business is, which means Alexandria is now getting a lot more competitors:

We’re seeing this concept of office-to-lab conversions gain traction across the U.S., particularly in markets where life sciences hubs intersect with world-class hospitals and leading universities. Cities like Boston, Philadelphia, New York, San Diego, and the Research Triangle in North Carolina are all attractive locations for life sciences companies looking to cluster near top talent from these academic and healthcare institutions—and that cluster effect often means we’re looking for lab conversion opportunities in very specific neighborhoods. - STO Building Group.

I am convinced that this is scaring investors away, causing Alexandria, which is a very healthy company with good management, to trade as if its business model is in danger.

This is reflected in analyst expectations.

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, analysts expect per-share adjusted funds from operations contraction of 4% this year, potentially followed by 5% contraction in 2026 and a potential recovery of 1% in 2027.

Although ARE trades at just 10.4x AFFO, a mile below its long-term average of 20.0x, I believe the industry has structural problems that do not make ARE a great investment, regardless of its elevated yield and past successes.

All things considered, ARE may recover in the future as industry dynamics improve. After all, it's not a bad company. I just think the odds of decent long-term returns are subdued, even at these levels.

Apple Inc. (AAPL): One Of My Least Favorite Big Tech Companies

I love Apple's products.

For example, I have a Mac Mini, a MacBook Air, an iPhone, and an iPad. The iPad is turning 10 years old this year, but still.

Apple products have always been a favorite of mine, as they are easy to use, look good, and tend to be very durable.

Unfortunately, I am no fan of its stock, as I believe Apple is in the process of transitioning from a growth stock to a value stock.

In general, it seems Apple and some of the other Big Tech companies are going different ways. For example, over the past four quarters, Apple's spending on capital expenditures was dwarfed by companies like Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Meta (META).

Comparing Apple to Microsoft below, we see that Microsoft is now spending more than 20% of its revenue on CapEx, a blowout number fueled by its ambitions to become a leader in global AI. Apple, meanwhile, spends just 3% of its revenue on CapEx, a number that's below its 10-year average.

Generally speaking, this does not mean doom and gloom.

However, it suggests that Apple is going in a different direction.

While the company had a great earnings report, with strength in key areas like its iPhone, it still heavily relies on these hardware products, as the iPhone accounts for roughly half of its revenue. This is a very saturated market, which mainly relies on replacement cycles without market share losses and growth in emerging markets, where a rising middle class could fuel demand.

Currently, there are close to 2.4 billion Apple devices around the world, which provides the company with a wide moat advantage. It comes with witching benefits due to its strong ecosystem. It seems to me that Apple is mainly hoping its competitors will come up with advanced AI tools that it can integrate into its own products.

It's not a bad strategy, but it makes the company dependent on others.

While the company has Apple Intelligence and plans to roll out a much better Siri next year, I have to say that its comments and AI efforts pale in comparison to what some of its peers offer:

[...] the fact that we are increasing our investment significantly in AI. So that is certainly a component of it. As you know, we've been investing in private cloud compute, which is also in our first-party data centers. The other piece, as you know, is that we do have a hybrid strategy where, in cases, we do use third parties to make capital investments, and we also invest in our own. So you are going to see an increase in CapEx. We also, from time to time, have other investments in facilities, in toolings, but I would say a significant portion of the driver of growth that you're seeing now is really driven by some of our AI-related investments. - AAPL Q3'25 Earnings Call

Although I believe that Apple will be successful in integrating third-party services and or even acquiring a fast-growing AI startup, I am not surprised that critics are speaking up:

[...] Apple’s top decision-makers recognize that something needs to change. Services chief Eddy Cue, one of Cook’s most trusted advisers, has even publicly warned within the company that if Apple doesn’t adjust quickly, it risks going the way of BlackBerry or Nokia. - Patently Apple.

As a result, I believe the company is slowly becoming a value stock.

However, this could come with a prolonged period of subdued returns, as Apple still has a lofty valuation, trading at 28.0x earnings, with expectations of mid-to-high single-digit annual EPS growth (FactSet). Its ten-year average P/E ratio is 23.5x.

Moreover, although Apple is expected to generate a whopping $138 billion in free cash flow by 2027, it would still translate to slightly less than 5.0% of its market cap, which would limit total annual shareholder distributions to the low-to-mid single digits, a number that is far from spectacular.

Its 0.5% yield doesn't change that.

This may explain why the ratio between AAPL and the S&P 500 (SP500) has gone sideways for roughly five years, something I expect to continue.

All things considered, as much as I love Apple's products, I do not believe it's a great place to be going forward.

T. Rowe Price Group, Inc. (TROW): There Are So Many Better Alternatives

Just like Alexandria Real Estate, many people have brought up T. Rowe Price in recent years, as it yields nearly 5.0% with a 55% payout ratio, a five-year CAGR of 8.6%, and a track record of almost 40 consecutive annual dividend hikes.

All else excluded, these numbers are fantastic!

Unfortunately, dividend growth has almost flatlined in recent years.

The same goes for its stock price.

Even if you had reinvested every penny of dividends, the total return of the past ten years would have been just 95%, one of the worst numbers among established financial corporations.

As much as I respect many of the company's asset managers, the asset management industry is under siege, and T. Rowe Price isn't immune.

Essentially, the relentless shift toward low-cost, passive investing, like Vanguard and BlackRock's (BLK) index funds and ETFs, has been eating away at active managers' market share, as we can clearly see below.

The company has seen five consecutive years of net capital outflows. Without elevated market returns in recent years, its assets under management would be far below the $1.7 trillion it has now.

As of June 2025, retirement assets account for 66% of its total AUM. Of its AUM, close to $600 billion consists of multi-asset solutions, an area under fire from increased competition. The same goes for general equity exposure.

Moreover, while T. Rowe Price is increasingly focused on ETFs.

We are also growing our ETF product line with the recent launch of 2 diverse equity ETFs, global equity and international equity research, and 3 sector ETFs, financials, health care, and natural resources. This brings our ETF range to 24, with more in the pipeline. We continue to expand private market alternatives in our wealth channel and evaluate the potential to bring private market alternatives to the retirement channel. - TROW Q2'25 Earnings Call.

However, so far, it has barely more than 20 ETFs, with most of them having elevated expense ratios of more than 0.3%, numbers that are (mostly) not competitive in a "BlackRock-dominated" world.

While I don't expect TROW to go out of business, it will have to pick a niche and focus on that, as playing catchup with BlackRock and others may not be the best thing to do, especially not because, so far, it has led to margin compression and the inability to grow net income despite massive market tailwinds.

So, although its yield seems attractive, I would rather buy a company in a different industry with a similar or higher yield and buy one of its peers like BlackRock, which has massively diverged from TROW since 2022, fueled by success in alternative investments, low-cost ETFs, and innovation in other areas to turn the company into a one-stop-shop for asset allocation needs.

Even worse, analysts do not expect earnings growth in 2025-2027, as we can see below, which means I do not expect the market to give TROW a higher multiple than the 11-12x it trades at now in the foreseeable future.

That's why I think the company is a strong case of "avoid" and a potential yield trap for dividend investors looking for a good deal.

Takeaway

As we discussed in the introduction, I would rather spend my time hunting for great stocks than warning people about bad ones, because most stocks simply are bad.

Research shows just 4% of companies drive half the market's wealth creation.

The rest of them are barely better (if not worse) than holding cash.

That said, sometimes it makes sense to assess some of the market's favorite stocks and the ones that people keep bringing up for a wide range of reasons, as satisfying past returns are no guarantee of future success.

I brought up three stocks:

Alexandria Real Estate has solid management and a 7% yield, but the healthcare/biolab real estate space is under tremendous pressure.

Apple makes products I love, but its stock is transitioning from a growth to a value profile, trading at a valuation that may not be justified by subdued growth and innovation risks.

Meanwhile, T. Rowe Price has a stellar dividend history, but active asset managers are losing ground to passive giants. Without growth, that 5% yield could be a trap.

Needless to say, I wish all of these companies and their investors nothing but the best and hope that I'm wrong on all three.

data.title: Tokyo Electron: Weak Short-Term Trends Mask Medium-Term Opportunities
data.description:
Intro

Tokyo Electron (TOELY) (TOELF) is a leading semiconductor equipment firm, having solid market shares in etching, wafer bonding, and a smaller presence in deposition and patterning. It is therefore exposed to a large part of the semiconductor supply chain. To gain a deeper understanding of the business foundation, please read my January initiation report. In this paper, I will focus on the most recent result, indicating near-term order book weaknesses. In the conference call, the management gave a set of reassuring comments on mid-term growth opportunities. They seem sufficiently credible. The stock valuation is attractive, especially after the drop post-result, and provides some margin of safety to add the position for patient investors. As a consequence, I keep my BUY rating on the stock.

Investment Thesis

Tokyo Electron just released its Q1FY26 financial report. The firm attributes the current market weakness as short-term, partially affected by tariff uncertainties. It sees its client focusing on and optimizing yields on the current installed base rather than making large new orders. The CEO said: “When it comes to investment projects, there have been no cancellations at all. Maybe the delay should be six months at most,” and also calls the Q4FY26 a potential activity bottom: « I think January to March is very close to the bottom ».

On top of that, the share of Chinese purchases has substantially decreased over the year, and this is seen more as a normalization post inventory build-up, fearing US and related tech sanctions. It was said: “We don’t see a further decline in the Chinese WFE market in the future”. So overall, a rather optimistic management tone, trying to see through the cycle.

Considering Tokyo Electron's competitive edge, the firm is confident it can continue to gain share in FY27, and said: « I think we can outperform the WFE market also in FY2027 ». The management reminded us that it outgrew the market significantly last year: +23% YoY versus a WFE market advancing by +6%. By client end markets, we see that memory remains an important vertical and that NAND flash (non-volatile memories) are starting to increase in the mix.

Trying to project into the medium-term, the CEO expressed optimism on the increasing semiconductor content in the next generation of chips. More specifically, the growth of high-bandwidth memories should increase with the new generation of AI accelerators (GPUs or ASICs). This should therefore translate into more demand for Tokyo Electron's equipment offering. Despite short-term weaknesses, the management said: “there is no change in our outlook of the semiconductor market, which keeps increasing, driven by new chips to be released by 2027”.

As a consequence, the CEO keeps its mid-term targets of reaching a revenue close to the “Yen 3 trillion or more” by 2027.

Concerning tangible investments, the firm just finished its Miyagi factory specializing in etching in April 2025. Another building focused on coater and bonder should be completed by the end of 2025. For FY26, the management anticipates R&D expenses of JPY295bn (US$2bn) and CAPEX of JPY240bn (US$1.6bn).

Tokyo Electron had $3.3bn of cash and equivalents during the last FY25 (ended in March 2025), and no debt as can be seen below.

What Valuation Can We Expect?

Using the main hypotheses:

  • A revenue of $20bn in FY27, close to the “Yen3tn or more” medium-term target of the management.
  • FY26 CAPEX should reach $1.6bn due to the completion of two new plants. I then expect a downward normalization for years to come.
  • FCF margins (FCF/revenues) are converging gradually toward 25%, in line with leading semiconductor equipment companies.
  • Share count: with a market cap of $67.13bn, and a market price of USD73/share; there is 920millions of share in the US listing.

With a terminal growth of 3% and a WACC of 9%, I get a target price of $101/share, indicating a 35% upside potential.

Risks

Tokyo Electron and most of its peers have experienced a disappointing YTD performance, as a consequence of rising geopolitical uncertainties: trade wars, tariff shocks, but also weak cyclical end-markets (auto and industrials). LAM Research (LAM) and KLAC, on the other hand, are more tilted toward AI applications, especially in the High bandwidth memory space, gaining traction.

On the weak side, peers like the lithography company ASML (ASML) showcased a shockingly weak FY26 guidance, as it is not sure it will be able to grow its sales. We can see that cyclical trends are not that clear, and we have to put that in perspective with Tokyo Electron’s rather optimistic comments on an activity bottom by the beginning of the calendar year 2026.

In terms of valuation, the firm is relatively cheap versus its peers according to my estimates, offering some margin of safety for investors.

Conclusion

Tokyo Electron has a quality portfolio of semiconductor equipment. Near-term cyclical weakness, partly related to trade frictions, should not prevent us from investing in a cheaply valued stock. It is time to add to the position after the recent stock drop. I keep my BUY rating on TOELY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: LendingClub: Raising Price Target After Blowout Quarter
data.description:
LendingClub (LC) delivered fantastic 2Q25 results, smashing through consensus estimates. Diluted EPS of $0.33 on total revenue of $248.4 million beat consensus estimates by $0.18 and $21 million, respectively.

LC, a digital bank that largely refinances consumer credit card debt, also achieved year-end internal goals more than six months in advance. Additionally, management raised the company's full-year guidance well ahead of analyst estimates, according to data from Visible Alpha:

As a result of the strong beat-and-raise, I am increasing my earnings per share estimates for 2025 and 2026, which is also driving my price target on the stock higher. With consumer debt at an all-time high and LC's loan programs starting to gain momentum, the company is well-positioned to generate higher, more sustainable earnings over time. Although the economic outlook remains uncertain, LC is flush with capital and has demonstrated over the past five years that it will not take unnecessary risks and can navigate a difficult economic environment.

Highlights in the quarter included strong credit performance, higher-than-expected originations, and strong marketing efficiency. CEO Scott Sanborn on the company's earnings call said the results represent "... an inflection point in both our strategic and our financial trajectory..." The stock rocketed higher after the positive results. The company guided for a 3Q25 return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) in the 10% to 11.5% range. Coming into the quarter, LC had projected an 8%+ ROTCE by 4Q25.

Management also said they expect fourth-quarter results to be similar to 3Q25, despite some seasonal headwinds to originations.

Notables In The Quarter

LC's originations of nearly $2.4 billion exceeded management's quarterly guidance of $2.3 billion at the top end of its range.

Originations were powered by very efficient marketing. Earlier this year, management expanded its marketing efforts and spending after pricing on loan sales improved, making the economics worthwhile to move into marketing channels like direct mail and online advertising. Heading into the quarter, I had projected that marketing expense as a percentage of total originations would increase to 1.57%. Marketing spend came in at 1.40%.

Credit also came in much better than anticipated, with a provision for credit losses of less than $40 million, the lowest level seen since 2Q24 and much better than consensus estimates.

In the first quarter, management took an $8.5 million qualitative provision for loans retained on the balance sheet, due to the tariff chaos in April. That was not reversed in 2Q25 and will ultimately be heavily dependent on the official Moody's forecast, which LC uses for its CECL provisioning for loans on its balance sheet. So if Moody's eventually improves its forecast, the $8.5 million could eventually be reversed and serve as a windfall to future earnings.

LC also closed its first transaction with BlackRock for $100 million of its investment-grade structured certificates (See here for more information on how the structured product works). Additionally, LC announced forward-flow agreements with Blue Owl for up to $3.4 billion, with an initial near-term commitment of $600 million, and for up to $1 billion with BlackRock.

Raising Estimates

Given the strong quarter and new guidance, I am raising my 2025 EPS estimate to $1.05 (from $0.72) and my 2026 EPS estimate to $1.39 (from $1.28).

Management will likely continue to lean into the structured product, including more sales to insurance firms. The company is also seeing strong demand for its loan seasoning program, in which it retains loans on the balance sheet for several months before selling them to investors. LC doesn't have to take a provision for seasoned loans (although it does mark them to fair value each quarter), and it provides investors with an opportunity to purchase a bundle of loans with better insight into their credit performance. Here is my current trajectory for quarterly originations:

3Q25 -- $2.55 billion

4Q25 -- $2.53

1Q26 -- $2.55

2Q26 -- $2.70

3Q26 -- $2.85

4Q26 -- $2.80

I realize my 2026 estimates may end up looking conservative, but I am baking in some economic uncertainty and keeping my interest rate assumptions conservative as well.

I am modeling for the net interest margin [NIM] to trend slightly lower over the next few quarters, largely due to deposit growth needed to fund loan growth, but still roughly around 2Q25 levels. I also expect NIM to surpass 6.30% by the back half of 2026.

Given continued uncertainty from tariffs and their impact on inflation, I am also dialing back my interest rate assumptions to only three rate cuts between now and the end of 2026. As of this writing, the forward curve is projecting five cuts.

If I were to follow the forward curve and model five interest rate cuts, the NIM would surpass 6.50% by the end of 2026, and my 2026 EPS would increase $0.17 to $1.56.

Another area in which I am being conservative is the credit provision. My assumptions include a lifetime loss rate of 8.4% in 2025 and 7.8% in 2026, which still brings my provision materially above consensus estimates in 2025 and 2026, according to Visible Alpha.

Despite using what I believe to be conservative assumptions in several key areas, I am still projecting for 2025 earnings to grow 132% year over year and 32% in 2026.

Risks

The big risk for LC is a major disruption to the economy or bond market, which could lead to declining investor interest in purchasing the company's loans.

Remember, LC typically balance sheets anywhere from 25% to 40% of its quarterly originations. These have the highest returns over their life, but are capital-intensive and require LC to provision up front for potential losses, which detracts from earnings.

The rest of LC's loans need to be purchased by investors, including banks, asset managers, insurance companies, or other forms of private credit. If the economy suddenly enters a deep recession and credit quality deteriorates, it becomes more difficult for LC to sell its loans. Additionally, rising short-term yields in the bond market result in a higher cost of capital for asset managers, which in turn leads them to demand higher returns on loan purchases.

There have also been many headlines about the growth of private credit and the quality of assets being bought. Investors typically perceive faster growth as riskier, and there is less regulatory oversight in private credit, although there certainly seems to be no shortage of capital coming into the space right now. However, any kind of systemic event, even if it has nothing to do with LC, could potentially spook investors.

The good news is that over the last five years, LC has navigated a pandemic and the most intense rising interest rate environment in about 40 years. That birthed the structured product and the extended seasoning programs, which should make LC more resilient through the economic cycle.

Deregulation in the banking sector, including lower regulatory capital requirements, could also position banks, the most resilient of LC's loan buyers, even better than in prior years.

Raising Price Target

In my working model, I project LC will exit 2026 with a ROTCE in the 11.5% to 12% range and a tangible book value per share of $13.43. If I use a 12% ROTCE, that would translate into a 120% premium to the company's tangible book value per share, valuing the stock at $16.12, slightly above where the stock trades now.

But using a ROTCE-driven TBV valuation is not entirely prudent for LC because the company operates with an inflated capital base. I have LC's common equity tier 1 capital ratio exiting 2026 above 18% with a tier 1 leverage ratio above 12%. These are incredibly high capital ratios for a bank. LC should operate with higher regulatory capital ratios than a typical bank because its main use case, involving consumer installment loans, is inherently riskier than other loan categories. But this kind of buffer is still excessive, although the strong capital position will allow LC to fund continued balance sheet growth without having to conduct dilutive capital raises.

On an earnings basis, LC trades at roughly 11.4x my projected 2026 EPS. Over the last five years, the average forward P/E multiple for the company has averaged close to 24x. Using that multiple would value the stock at about $33.30.

Applying a 60-40 weighting to both price targets, with the heavier weight going to the ROTCE-driven TBV price target, I arrive at a price target of about $23 per share (up from $15.73 in my last write-up), assuming no extreme economic events.

data.title: Royalty Pharma: Keep Delivering, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
Royalty Pharma plc (NASDAQ:RPRX) has reported its latest results. The company's performance strongly surpassed our expectations, reinforcing our view that it is well-positioned for an even brighter future.

For new readers, Royalty Pharma is the world’s largest acquirer of biopharmaceutical royalties by transaction value. Its model is straightforward, though it requires some pharma and finance knowledge: the company buys royalties that generate payments tied directly to the top‑line sales of leading therapies. Since our strong buy recommendation (Massive Opportunity Ahead, Nov 2024), RP's shares have risen by almost 48% (including the dividend payment, Fig. 1).

Aside from a valuation gap, our buy was backed by 1) RP's ability to generate FCF, 2) sound capital allocation priorities (M&A first, dividend increase year-on-year, and a $3 billion buyback), and 3) a corporate structure simplification. On the downside, we also highlighted the Vertex franchise development.

Fig 1

Q2 Results and Our Positive Take

Royalty Pharma’s revenue stream is straightforward. Royalty receipts rose 11% to $672 million (Fig. 2), with an adjusted EBITDA increase of 13%. In this quarter, the company also reported a sales milestone of $56 million. For the RP business nature (with no operating costs), this led to an EPS beat and a higher cash flow generation. Milestone and other contractual payments are excluded from our guidance due to these inflows' limited visibility and inherent unpredictability.

On the core business (Fig. 2), RP reported higher royalties from Evrysdi (+32%), Tremfya (+24%), and Cabometyx/Cometriq (+22%). In number, the Q2 company's portfolio cash flow increased by 12% to $562 million.

Source: Royalty Pharma Q2 press release, Fig 2

Why are we positive?

Our investment thesis on Royalty Pharma is playing out as anticipated, and we can confidently check the boxes on our key investment pillars. We now highlight two further upside catalysts:

  1. RP communicated the RP Management acquisition. This will combine the RP portfolio with manager IP, leading to a unique enterprise. Thus reducing the cost structure. In the FY 2025 guidance, the company is already reporting 50/100 basis points of savings in professional costs (Fig. 3);
  2. We can report solid execution with outstanding royalty growth year-on-year. While growth seems to moderate in %, the company is executing billion‑dollar transactions, which should underpin future earnings expansion and provide greater visibility. The company communicated a new funding agreement with Revolution Medicines (Fig. 4) for an initial value of $1.25 billion. Daraxonrasib is a multi-selective inhibitor in Phase III development and cures mutant pancreatic cancer and non-small cell lung cancer. This is a multi-billion-dollar blockbuster (Fig. 5) with potential royalties for the next 15 years;
  3. Aside from the new royalties, the company continues to remunerate its shareholders based on. Aside from the DPS increase, the company bought back 8 million shares for a total value of $277 million. There was a $1 billion repurchase in H1, with a $3 billion target. Thanks to MorphoSys' disposal and a strong FCF, the company still has $632 in cash.

On the new upside: 1) the company raised its full-year guidance (this is also supported by Milestones' contractual agreement with almost $110 million in sales in 2025), and the portfolio receipts are expected to increase by at least +2.5% (Fi. 3) and 2) during the earnings call, the CEO mentioned upside from Chinese royalties.

Although not directly tied to Royalty Pharma, one of the latest additions to our portfolio is an investment in Arrivent Biopharma. The company completed an exclusive licensing agreement with Shanghai Allist Pharmaceuticals to sell its future product worldwide except in China.

Royalty and licensing deals in China typically involve lower upfront costs and higher royalty rates, which could present an opportunity for additional upside for Royalty Pharma.

Source: Royalty Pharma Q2 ppt - Fig 3

Fig 4

Fig 5

Earnings Changes and Valuation

In our last analysis, we increased our portfolio receipts growth to 9% with top-line sales of $3.1 billion. This estimate is now aligned with RP's latest updated guidance. On the EBITDA, we reported RP has a limited cost structure, but this year, they incorporated RP Management, and with MorphoSys sales, RP recorded $70 million of one-off expenses, so our EBITDA moved down from $2.85 billion to $2.78 billion. With no D&A, our interest payment also increased to $275 million. With no corporate tax, our earnings reached $2.50 billion. Considering the 583 million shares outstanding, our EPS moved to $4.28.

Applying an unchanged P/E of 10x (aligned with its 5-year historical average—Fig. 6), our target price reached $42.8 with a 14% upside potential. We should consider the negative one-off nature of the higher operating costs and $15 million impact from interest rates, which are linked to a mismatch between funding and royalties. Our upside also does not consider:

  1. With a $2 billion buyback, the company might redeem at least 9.2% of the current outstanding shares.
  2. "That other royalty companies usually trade at a premium compared to their respective sector."

Fig 6

Risks

Downside risks include 1) worse than anticipated performance of current royalties, 2) new corporate tax, 3) higher interest rates, 4) value destruction in the royalties deal, 5) higher comps in the royalty investing space, and 6) lower synergies to RP Management. Still related to our negative note on Vertex's potential arbitration results, we report the following update from RP's press release (Fig. 7). This news could mark a negative precedent in Royalty Pharma’s history, potentially resulting in higher legal costs ahead.

Fig 7

Conclusion

With a 14% upside to our one‑year target, supported by a buyback program and a coupon yield above 6% annually, we remain buyers. Our target price already factors in one‑off downside more than it does in future upside opportunities.

data.title: AIO: Trades At A Premium Valuation Despite Flaws
data.description:
Overview

I've always been fascinated by funds that can provide exposure to some of the most innovative companies in the world, while simultaneously providing investors with a high level of income. Virtus Artificial Intelligence & Technology Opportunities Fund (AIO) strikes a balance between growth and income, and I believe that the efficient portfolio strategy makes this fund a great long-term holding. I previously covered AIO and issued a buy rating due to the portfolio strategy, strong performance, and healthy dividend coverage. I wanted to revisit the fund to assess its most updated annual report and provide insights into its valuation.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, AIO's share price has increased by about 24.6%. The fund experienced a large decline in the first quarter of the year alongside the rest of the market, but thankfully AIO was capable of riding the recovery momentum over recent months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return increases to 34.3% over the same period. AIO is interesting because it provides investors with a generous starting dividend yield of 7.3%.

However, the fund still trades at a slight premium to NAV, and there are some risks that investors should be aware of. For instance, AIO is likely to underperform more traditional index-tracking ETFs that can provide a greater level of capital appreciation. Additionally, AIO's level of net investment income has been extremely weak in recent years. The fund offers very little downside protection, and the distributions may not be sustainable during periods where market indexes are moving sideways or declining. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that AIO implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, AIO has total managed assets of $941M that are spread across 96 different positions. The fund aims to focus on holdings that are embracing disruptive AI innovations and technologies, while also including exposure to relevant hardware companies. However, AIO takes a blended exposure approach by including some income securities alongside traditional common equities. The breakdown is as follows:

  • Common Equities: 52.19%
  • Convertible Securities: 29.31%
  • High Yield Bonds: 15.69%
  • Cash and equivalents: 2.81%

Looking at the sector allocation of the fund's equities, we can see that AIO is primarily focused on Information Technology companies, which make up 45.44% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Financials and Consumer Discretionary investments, which account for 11.51% and 9.31% of assets, respectively. However, investors should anticipate that AIO's technology holdings will have the greatest impact on the daily price change and total return potential.

The fund provides exposure to many high-quality large-cap companies. According to the top holdings, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest holding at 5.13%. This is followed by exposure to other large-cap holdings like Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), Flex (FLEX), and JPMorgan (JPM) just to name a few. However, we must remember that AIO also has exposure to fixed-income securities within, so the fund may not fully participate in the growth of the market.

Updated Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, AIO traded at a slight premium to NAV of 6.75%. Due to the recent market uncertainty, AIO's premium to NAV slightly declined to approximately 3.45%. However, the current premium valuation still sits well above AIO's average price to NAV discount of 6.1% over the last three-year period. In an effort to help visualize AIO's current valuation, I added the red line in the graph below. As we can see, AIO's current valuation sits well above its average range since the fund's inception.

In an effort to determine whether or not the premium valuation is justified, I reviewed the most updated annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.01 per share, while net realized gains were $4.70 per share. Looking back on prior years, net investment income levels were consistently low. The low net investment income levels may be attributed to the primary focus on technology holdings, but I also hope to see major improvements here since the fund also has exposure to income producing convertible securities. This poor performance in terms of net investment income has caused AIO to be very reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support distributions.

The only issue with this dependency on net realized gains is that AIO offers no protection during market declines. As you can imagine, it can be difficult for a fund to capture net realized gains during periods where market indexes are declining. For instance, AIO recognized a large net realized loss in 2023, due to the market decline experienced throughout 2022. A higher level of net investment income may make the fund more resilient during market declines and help protect the NAV. Thankfully, AIO experienced a strong year of growth during 2024, which helped the NAV increase at the close of this report. The year-end NAV amounts are as follows:

  • 2020: $19.89 per share
  • 2021: $29.20 per share
  • 2022: $24.18 per share
  • 2023: $19.92 per share
  • 2024: $20.61 per share
  • 2025: $23.52 per share

Since the fund doesn't produce a meaningful amount of income, it essentially means that AIO is selling off its upside in order to prioritize distributions. Therefore, I want to put emphasis on the fact that AIO is best utilized by the investors that specifically want to focus on income. If you are an investor that wants to pursue the highest possible total return within your portfolio, you will likely be better off with a more traditional index-tracking ETF.

Risk Profile

Since AIO needs to sell off portions of its securities as a way to fuel growth. The fund is essentially sacrificing price appreciation for income generation. Therefore, I believe that investors with a longer time horizon ahead may be better off with a more traditional approach, such as Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). To demonstrate AIO's weakness, I pulled in a total return comparison of AIO versus QQQ, since AIO's inception. As we can see below, QQQ outpaces AIO in total return, despite AIO offering a much higher starting dividend yield of 7.3%.

I am very bullish on the outlook of the technology sector due to the evolving nature of different artificial intelligence products and services. I already own a pair of META's AI glasses, frequently use Google's (GOOGL) (GOOG) Gemini LLM, and continue to see different use cases for varying forms of AI. I strongly believe that AI will continue to be a large growth driver for businesses across the world, as they evolve how we work, interact with different forms of entertainment, and influence the tools we use for daily life.

Despite AIO providing exposure to the very same companies that have some form of exposure to the AI market, the fund is not a great choice for participating in the AI expansion. For instance, Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034. Since AIO will limit the level of capital appreciation experienced, investors may be leaving money on the table by prioritizing income instead of growth.

Lastly, the fund implements leverage as a part of its operating strategy, and this can increase the risk profile. The use of leverage is typically used as a way to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity is present. The amount of leverage being used sits around 13.62% of assets, which is equivalent to $130M. I consider this to be a moderate amount of leverage, but there may still be some negative consequences. For instance, investors must consider the risk that leverage is allocated to investments that don't perform as expected, which can actually amplify losses instead.

Dividend Sustainability

According to the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.15 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.3%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.80 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, AIO has an inconsistent history of being able to support payouts. Just for reference, here are the year-end earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $0.22 per share
  • 2021: $11.80 per share
  • 2022: ($0.17) per share
  • 2023: ($2.46) per share
  • 2024: $2.49 per share
  • 2025: $4.71 per share

As we can see, AIO has been able to generate earnings that outpace the distribution amount in three of the last five years. While AIO has no issue generating positive earnings during periods where market indexes are trending upward, I am cautious about the fund's sustainability during prolonged periods of decline. For instance, let's suppose that market indexes remain flat for the next two years as a result of tariffs. As it stands, AIO's usual net investment income levels aren't sufficient enough to support payouts on their own, and if market indexes aren't increasing, AIO may not be able to generate enough gains to support NAV growth and distributions.

However, I wouldn't necessarily worry yet. AIO has been able to produce large enough earnings on its positive years, that can support multiple years of distributions. For instance, 2025's total earnings of $4.71 per share are enough to support an annual distribution of $1.8 per share for more than two and a half years of time. Therefore, AIO is likely to keep payouts the same at this time, and I wouldn't anticipate a cut.

Additionally, the fund seems to prioritize making distributions in a tax-efficient manner. For instance, the most recent Section 19(a) notice indicates that 89.4% of the distributions YTD have been paid out using long-term capital gains, which have favorable tax consequences for investors. Assuming the fund can maintain payouts with a similar breakdown, AIO can be a great choice for retirees that want to collect a high rate of income without worrying about the tax burden.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on AIO at this time. I like the fund's structure and strategy, but there are clear areas that can be improved. For instance, the level of net investment income needs to be improved, especially for a fund that puts a clear emphasis on income generation. The weak net investment income levels make AIO reliant on net realized gains, which makes the fund more vulnerable to market declines. Additionally, the price still trades at a premium valuation in relation to its usual range over prior years. The focus on income generation also means that AIO is likely to underperform more straight-forward ETFs like QQQ over the long term.

data.title: IEF: Staying Bullish As Spread Repricing Echoes Opportunity
data.description:
The iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:IEF) is back in focus today. We last covered the fund in May, reiterating our bullish outlook on the basis of U.S. Treasury yields presenting solid return opportunities. Since then, yields have dropped to circa 420 - 425 basis points, IEF has generated a total return of around 3%. However, additional material factors have emerged, allowing us to re-engage with the IEF; herewith is a summary of our updated thoughts.

Real Macro Variables (A Cyclical Outlook)

Let's re-engage with a few real macro variables to set a base for our cyclical outlook. The following diagram communicates a few critical points; a discussion follows.

Variable Output
Quarterly Real GDP growth 3%
Output Gap 13.33%
Annual PCE Change 2.6%
CPI Y/Y 2.7%
Consumer Sentiment 60.7
JOLTS 7.437M
Unemployment Rate 4.25%
Non-Farm Payrolls +73K

Source: Trading Economics, Investing.com, YCharts - Periodicity may differ among variables

The U.S.' GDP grew at an annualised rate of 3% in Q2 after slumping by -0.5% in Q2. We don't think these figures are particularly informative. Most of Q1's slowing seemingly resulted from a surge in tariff-driven imports, which depleted the net exports line item in the GDP calculation. The opposite occurred in Q2, allowing real GDP to settle higher – We think looking at sub-variables as determinants of the U.S.'s cyclical growth outlook would be more beneficial than observing headline GDP figures.

The primary factors we've decided to track include: the labor market, PCE, and consumer sentiment. In our view, cyclical growth will mostly depend on consumer sentiment and business' confidence to invest. Moreover, we think Federal Reserve decisions will have a significant impact on sentiment, and therefore, we deem labor market and inflationary data critical.

As matters stand, consumers have lost confidence, which we anticipated to occur a while back, largely due to cyclical economic effects (the output gap it arguably overcooked), a higher-for-longer interest rate environment, and the loss in trade confidence amid an unstable foreign policy environment. Recent data releases have aligned with a view we've echoed on Seeking Alpha for numerous months. For example, job openings data remains in a downtrend, labor force participation is stuck below pre-COVID levels, non-farm payrolls missed forecasts (yes, I'm aware of data quality concerns), and consumer sentiment is under pressure. Reported PCE and CPI have both trended higher post-liberation day; however, we see the variables being sticky – short-term price hikes -> short-to-intermediate-term impact on consumers -> substitution emerges and lower consumer sentiment consolidates -> limitations imposed on companies' ability to pass input costs through to their end markets. Unfortunately, the Federal Reserve has been placed in a position where inflation uncertainty restricts its base case for target rate cuts. Considering all these variables, we see the following pathway unfolding: growth slows organically, the Federal Reserve acts slower-than anticipated, growth and inflation both taper to their lower boundaries, leading the bond market into pricing a series of rate cuts throughout 2026. Additionally, we see investors turning risk-off, providing the treasury market with additional support.

To close this section, I'd like to add a few additional notes on inflation:

I want to mention two additional matters regarding inflation. First, many have referred to tariffs lately while ignoring oil prices. WTI has settled in the mid-$60 range and its curve is in backwardation, a significant improvement from the $120 peak in 2022. Secondly, it's worth noting that the Fed emphasizes PCE, not just CPI – PCE accounts for substitution, making it a critical indicator at this time.

Curve and Spreads Analysis

The short-to-intermediate part of the U.S. Treasury Yield curve has continued to level down in recent months, ignoring temporary increases in CPI and PCE. In isolation, the short-to-intermediate part of the curve anticipates lower future interest rates, likely due to a slower growth outlook, softer inflation, or anomalous factors. In contrast, the longer-end of the curve has stayed elevated; whether due to re-growth expectations, a higher fiscal risk premium, or anomalous factors, remains debatable.

The spread between the 10-year nominal yield and the 3-month nominal yield acts as a useful barometer for cyclical measures. The spread has consolidated around the zero midpoint in recent weeks and within the +/- 25 basis point boundary in recent months. Conventionally, the consolidated level would signal mild potential for a recession. In our view, the current consolidated level signals a cyclical slowdown as opposed to a hard landing; a sustained level below -50 basis points would likely confirm a strong base case for a pending recession.

Though a useful cyclical tool, the 10Y-2Y signals policy pathways more than cyclical outlooks. The 10Y-2Y has consolidated around the 50 basis point level in recent months. In our view, the level suggests policy uncertainty, rather than clear conviction on Fed rate target ranges, most likely due to the unknowns introduced by a fiscal regime change, a monetary regime change, and the, AI as a productivity growth variable, debate. Alternatively, we could compare the 2Y-3M spread and speculate that the market believes the Fed will hold rates strong, leading to diminished cyclical growth – this comparison in itself can predict a recession but it's probably less meticulous than the 10Y-3M.

In our opinion, sustained signs of a cyclical slowdown and policy uncertainty would provide support to IEF ETF, which we largely base on a risk-off narrative. Keep in mind that, as a Treasury vehicle, IEF is priced in advance, meaning, barring autocorrelation, IEF is priced based on where the market believes future nominal rates will settle; it's not priced contemporaneously. So, even if the Fed keeps rates high and even if PCE remains sticky, we argue that future rates need to be isolated.

Real Rates, Breakevens, and The Term Premium

Real rates have struggled to find direction. Breakevens have found calm but remain susceptible to tariff noise. Keep in mind that breakevens price inflation uncertainty, not just the price level. Our view is that real rates will settle slightly lower in the coming quarters, which we base on a lower neutral rate (here's a link leading to a study of neutral rates by PIMCO), calming of the fiscal risk premium, and investor risk-off preference buffering against a potential productivity drag. Similarly, we think breakevens will taper slightly as the inflationary impact of tariffs becomes clearer. In addition, as argued before, we think the Fed's current hold on rates and the softening demand side of the economy will lead to the market pricing lower ex-ante inflation.

I wanted to isolate a study of the term premium to provide context on duration. The term premium spiked since late 2024. Some might suggest that the spike has resulted from re-growth expectations (after Fed target rates dropped), others may argue that the higher premium is a result of fiscal risks. We aren't quite sure about the term premium's year-to-date attribution. However, by eyeballing it, we'd say that there was an overreaction on regrowth. Moreover, we think the fiscal premium is justified, but many of the factors mentioned in the Moody's (MCO) downgrade report were likely already priced in. As for now, we remain concerned about issuances—the U.S. is issuing quite rampantly, which could drive term premiums higher throughout the curve, given that the U.S. already possesses an elevated debt level. That being said, we think the term premium will consolidate in the 40s if it receives a kicker from risk-off preference and once media noise about U.S. fiscal pressures fade.

As a judgement call, we'd price our six-month nominal 10-year yield target between a range of 390 and 410 basis points with a preference for the lower end. If realized, we could see IEF deliver valuable price returns–we probably won't see it sacrificing much yield, given that its portfolio's turnover (into new lower-yielding securities) would likely lag.

IEF's Key Metrics Revisited

IEF's metrics have altered by a slight amount since our last coverage. For example, its 30-day SEC yield has shrunk from 4.18% to 4.10%. Moreover, its average yield-to-maturity has decreased to 4.09% from the previous 4.19% we reported in May. IEF's duration and convexity metrics have stayed largely unchanged, but its option-adjusted spread has risen to 2.23 basis points from the 1.62 basis points conveyed in May.

In terms of distributions. Remember, IEF distributes its interest income. To our observation, IEF hasn't distributed capital gains nor returns of capital. The vehicle's return structure conveys its distribution profile, showing no signs of value destruction while simultaneously communicating a tendency to distribute its interest income instead of reinvesting the proceeds.

Risks Worth Considering

Credit Risk Being Assigned To U.S. Sovereigns

You might've noticed that IEF's option-adjusted spread has picked up to 2.23 basis points from 1.62 basis points since our latest coverage. The change could be due to portfolio composition. However, systematic influence wouldn't be a coincidence. The U.S. 10-year CDS spread has drifted structurally higher in recent years, most likely due to increased debt levels, fiscal dominance, insurance activity (CDS insurance contracts), and higher interest expenses. Since April 2023, the 10-year CDS has reached the 50 level on three occasions—we could see investors price credit risk into the U.S.' sovereigns if current CDS levels persist, in turn, influencing IEF's discount rates.

Fiscal Dominance

The U.S. is under fiscal dominance, whereby the government's spending focus has threatened the Federal Reserve's independence. Although the Fed has remained steadfast in its goals to drag inflation back to a region of stability, the lingering threats of fiscal dominance might add an unwanted risk premium to U.S. Treasuries and IEF alike.

Media Noise

Anecdotally, we think the media has made quite a lot of noise by reiterating the U.S.' fiscal concerns and the Trump administration's policy shifts. We think most of the media's concerns refer to known factors; astute bond investors probably priced fiscal strain, Japan funding costs, and President Trump's restrictive trade and relaxed tax policy beliefs in advance. Sure, the scale of tariff policies came as a shock; however, even that seems to be old news.

Having said that, the media's persistent focus on negative issues could lead some to divest from U.S. Treasuries, and in turn, devalue IEF.

Our Outlook May Simply Be Erroneous

Our outlook is merely our own. Interest rates are highly speculative by nature, and therefore we acknowledge the possibility of being wrong.

IEF Isn't A Hold-to-Maturity Asset

IEF isn't a direct held-to-maturity investment. It sells and buys treasuries to maintain a specific duration target. As such, the ETF's advertised yields may never materialize. In our view, IEF remains a cyclical trade as opposed to a long-term investment opportunity.

Our IEF Total Return Outlook (Six Months)

IEF has delivered a year-to-date total return of roughly 5.95%, which is well-beyond its since-inception annualised return of 3.48%. Based on our analysis, we expect the ETF to deliver excess returns by year-end. In our six-month outlook, we think IEF could deliver an additional 1.50%–2.00% in price returns and 1.6%–1.8% in distribution income; we range that to a potential return of between 3.00% and 3.50% (not by simple addition).

Herewith is a brief explanation of our return outlook.

  • As discussed earlier in the article, we expect the 10-year yield to sacrifice another 20–30 basis points within the next six months. By multiplying the ETF's current duration by 25 basis points, we derive a return target of 1.75%. We set the range at 1.50%-2.00% to remain conservative. Moreover, we wanted to account for the ETF's sub-10-year duration and potential portfolio composition adjustments.
  • The distribution return component was calculated by adjusting IEF's current weighted average coupon to a semi-annual figure, whereby a judgement call was made to account for fluctuations in IEF's portfolio composition.
  • Adding the higher ends (or lower ends) of the two variables together to establish a lower (or upper) boundary would be erroneous as yield and price adjustments aren't symmetrical. Therefore, we compressed our target range to account for the odd relationship between yield interest rate sensitivity and price-based interest rate sensitivity.

We are quite confident about our outlook. However, note that this sub-section provides an informal judgement call instead of a formal return model. Moreover, as previously mentioned, our rate outlook is speculative, it doesn't provide certainty; IEF's max drawdown of 23.92% illustrates what can happen when rate speculation goes wrong.

Conclusion

We hold the view that iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF's run is far from over. The ETF has delivered slid returns since the turn of the year, and we think additional total returns of between 3.00% and 3.50% (for the next six months) are on the cards. Our basis primarily derives from our 10-year yield target, which is nearer to the 400 basis point level. Moreover, we think IEF's portfolio will sustain its off-the-run distribution yield for an adequate amount of time to see it benefit from price returns without sacrificing much yield.

Although risks such as persistent fiscal dominance, IEF's cyclicality, and inflationary uncertainty remain, we are net bullish for the time being.

Consensus: Bullish
